name: Young Adult
description: Stories aimed at teen readers, typically centering identity formation, social belonging, first love, and boundary-testing choices. (This graph has 18 nodes — one more than the 17-node standard — because it includes a second Anti-Pattern node to capture an additional genre-specific pitfall.)

nodes.label: Young Adult Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised stories in which a young protagonist navigates the turbulent crossing from childhood into selfhood â€” where identity, belonging, first experiences, and contested authority produce choices that feel absolutely world-defining from inside the protagonist's perspective.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as Young Adult
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
typical_variants: Contemporary realism centering social and identity pressures
Speculative or dystopian settings externalizing those pressures
Romance as the primary arena for self-discovery
Adventure or fantasy as rite-of-passage structure
failure_modes: Promise of teen perspective but delivering adult retrospective judgment on teen experience
Genre promise without the urgent sincerity that defines YA emotional register
signals_in_text: Protagonist is demonstrably in the process of becoming
Stakes are framed in terms of who the protagonist is and will be
Emotional intensity is treated as valid rather than excessive

nodes.label: Protagonist Must Be in the Process of Identity Formation
nodes.definition: The central character must not yet know who they are â€” they are actively constructing a self, testing values, and discovering what they believe, want, and are willing to defend. Identity formation is not backstory; it is the live event of the narrative.
exit_conditions: Protagonist's sense of self is in visible flux and is transformed by story events
typical_variants: Discovery of a previously suppressed identity (sexuality, heritage, belief)
Rejection of an inherited identity (family role, expected future)
Forging an identity under external pressure to conform
failure_modes: Protagonist has a fully settled identity from the start and merely applies it
Identity shift is declared by narration rather than dramatized through choice and consequence
signals_in_text: Protagonist questions assumptions about themselves in early scenes
Key scenes hinge on who the protagonist decides to be rather than what they decide to do
Internal monologue or close-third narration tracks the instability of self-perception

nodes.label: First Experiences Carry Disproportionate Emotional Weight
nodes.definition: In YA, firsts â€” first love, first betrayal, first public failure, first moral compromise, first act of courage â€” are not merely plot events; they are orienting experiences that restructure the protagonist's understanding of the world. The narrative must honor this weight rather than minimize it.
exit_conditions: At least one first experience is staged and treated with the full emotional gravity it carries for the protagonist
typical_variants: First romantic relationship and its attendant intensity
First experience of systemic injustice or adult hypocrisy
First act of deliberate self-definition against external expectation
failure_modes: First experiences treated as trivial or obvious by narrator or other characters
Adult perspective imposed retroactively ('you'll understand later') deflating the present weight
signals_in_text: Narrative slows and expands around first-experience scenes
Protagonist's body and senses are foregrounded â€” physical specificity signals emotional intensity
The experience is described as if nothing like it has happened before, because for this character nothing has

nodes.label: Authority Structures Must Be Visible and Tested
nodes.definition: Parents, schools, social institutions, and systemic rules must appear as real forces in the story â€” not as neutral scenery but as entities that exert pressure, demand conformity, or obstruct the protagonist's self-determination. These structures must also be tested: challenged, questioned, circumvented, or in some cases rightfully accepted.
exit_conditions: At least one authority structure is actively engaged as a force the protagonist must navigate or resist
typical_variants: Parental authority â€” protective, controlling, or absent
Institutional authority â€” school rules, government systems, religious structures
Social authority â€” peer hierarchies, popularity codes, community norms
failure_modes: Authority figures are cardboard villains with no internal logic, making the conflict trivial
Authority is mentioned but never actually constrains the protagonist
All authority is validated â€” protagonist simply learns to obey
signals_in_text: Adult characters have agendas that conflict with the protagonist's
Rules are enforced with real consequences
Protagonist must decide whether a given authority deserves compliance or resistance

nodes.label: Choices Must Feel World-Defining from the Protagonist's Perspective
nodes.definition: Every major choice the protagonist makes must feel â€” from inside their experience â€” as if the entire shape of their life hinges on it. The narrative must sustain and honor this subjective absoluteness, even when an adult reader might recognize the choice as one of many. The genre contract requires that the protagonist's perspective governs the emotional register.
exit_conditions: Major choices are staged with the full weight they carry for a person experiencing them for the first time
typical_variants: A romantic choice that feels like the only possible love
A moral choice that feels like a permanent verdict on the protagonist's character
A social choice that feels like the end or beginning of belonging
failure_modes: Narrator or adult characters relativize the choice's importance mid-scene
Choices are presented as obviously correct, eliminating genuine tension
The story implies the protagonist is overreacting to events that really are trivial
signals_in_text: Interiority around choices is expansive and emotionally saturated
Time slows in decision moments
Consequences are framed in terms of identity, not just outcome

nodes.label: YA Contemporary Realism
nodes.definition: YA set in the recognizable present, where the arenas of high school, family, romance, mental health, sexuality, race, and class provide the material for identity formation. The world operates by familiar rules, and the drama emerges from human and social dynamics rather than speculative premises.
entry_conditions: Setting is the recognizable contemporary world
exit_conditions: Social and relational dynamics are the primary engine of conflict and growth
typical_variants: Coming-out story
Social mobility or class collision story
Mental health or chronic illness story
Friendship-to-rivalry or rivalry-to-friendship arc
failure_modes: Realism becomes an excuse for low stakes â€” nothing feels consequential
Contemporary setting treated as mere backdrop rather than active social pressure system
signals_in_text: Specific, named social textures â€” school lunch tables, texts, social media dynamics, family dinner conversations
Conflict rooted in who knows what about whom and who belongs where

nodes.label: YA Dystopian / Speculative
nodes.definition: YA in a speculative or dystopian world where external systems â€” oppressive governments, magical hierarchies, post-apocalyptic social orders â€” literalize and externalize the pressures of identity formation, authority, and belonging that are internal in contemporary realism. The speculative premise must remain tethered to the YA emotional core.
entry_conditions: Setting operates by speculative or altered rules
exit_conditions: Speculative system creates conditions that force the protagonist's identity formation and authority confrontation
typical_variants: Oppressive regime the protagonist must resist or escape
Sorting or classification system that assigns identity
Post-catastrophe world requiring a new moral order to be built
failure_modes: World-building crowds out character interiority â€” protagonist becomes a plot function
Speculative premise is decorative rather than generating genuine YA identity pressure
signals_in_text: The system's rules directly constrain the protagonist's self-determination
Resistance to the system is simultaneously self-discovery
Other characters represent different responses to the same systemic pressure

nodes.label: YA Romance
nodes.definition: YA in which romantic feeling is the primary arena for identity formation and first experiences. The relationship is not merely a subplot but the central lens through which the protagonist comes to understand themselves, desire, vulnerability, and the risks of genuine connection.
entry_conditions: A romantic relationship or longing is the central emotional engine
exit_conditions: The romantic arc produces genuine self-knowledge for the protagonist, not just relationship resolution
typical_variants: First love that ends in loss but produces growth
First love that survives at cost of significant self-disclosure
Love triangle as identity mirror â€” who the protagonist chooses reflects who they are becoming
failure_modes: Romance is wish-fulfillment without risk â€” protagonist gets the relationship and it costs them nothing
Love interest is purely a prize rather than a person who challenges the protagonist's self-concept
signals_in_text: Romantic scenes are also scenes of self-revelation
Vulnerability in romance tracks to vulnerability in the protagonist's broader identity formation
Obstacles are rooted in the protagonist's unresolved identity questions, not just external circumstance

nodes.label: YA Adventure / Fantasy
nodes.definition: YA structured around a quest, journey, or fantasy world in which the physical and magical challenges of adventure externalize the internal challenges of growing up. Discovery of power, lineage, or destiny is a metaphor for the identity formation at YA's core.
entry_conditions: Plot is organized around a quest, journey, or fantasy conflict
exit_conditions: The adventure's resolution produces genuine identity transformation, not just plot resolution
typical_variants: Chosen-one narrative where the protagonist must accept or reject the assigned identity
Quest that strips away false identities to reveal the true self
Found-family adventure where belonging is earned through shared trial
failure_modes: Adventure is so externally focused that character interiority is lost
Identity transformation is assigned by prophecy rather than earned through choice
signals_in_text: Physical challenges mirror internal stakes
Companions reflect different possible selves the protagonist could become
The climactic confrontation requires the protagonist to commit to a version of themselves

nodes.label: School / Social Hierarchy as Primary Arena
nodes.definition: Whether the setting is a literal school, a dystopian academy, a court, or a ship, the social hierarchy of the protagonist's peer world functions as the primary arena where identity is tested and belonging is contested. Status, reputation, and social position carry narrative weight.
exit_conditions: The social hierarchy exerts real pressure on the protagonist's choices and self-perception
typical_variants: Literal high school with visible clique structure
Fantasy court or guild as social hierarchy analog
Rebellion faction with its own internal hierarchy
failure_modes: Social hierarchy is mentioned but never actually affects the protagonist's options or standing
All characters treat the hierarchy as irrelevant, making the setting inert
signals_in_text: Scenes establish who sits where, who speaks to whom, who is visibly excluded
Protagonist's position in the hierarchy shifts as a consequence of identity choices
Status gains and losses are felt and registered by other characters

nodes.label: Peer Relationships Define the Social World
nodes.definition: Friends, rivals, love interests, and frenemies are the primary relational fabric of the story. Adult relationships matter but the world the protagonist lives in moment-to-moment is constituted by peer dynamics â€” loyalty, betrayal, attraction, exclusion, and solidarity among near-equals.
exit_conditions: Peer relationships are the primary site of emotional investment and narrative tension
typical_variants: Best friend whose loyalty is tested by the protagonist's changing identity
Rival who mirrors the protagonist's fears about their own worst self
Love interest who sees the protagonist more clearly than anyone else does
failure_modes: Adult relationships are more emotionally central than peer ones, shifting the genre register toward adult drama
Peers are undifferentiated background rather than specific individuals with their own arcs
signals_in_text: Peer conversations carry more narrative weight than family or authority conversations
Friend-group dynamics shift visibly across the story
The protagonist's standing among peers is a live emotional barometer

nodes.label: Adult World as Both Shelter and Cage
nodes.definition: The adult world in YA must function ambivalently â€” it provides the protagonist with real protection, resources, and care (shelter) while also constraining, misunderstanding, or threatening to absorb or erase the protagonist's emerging self (cage). Neither pure villain nor pure ally, the adult world is the complex backdrop against which the protagonist must define themselves.
exit_conditions: Adult characters and institutions are shown in both their protective and constraining dimensions
typical_variants: Well-meaning parent whose protection has become suffocating
Corrupt institution that once provided genuine community but now demands loyalty to its abuses
Mentor who empowers the protagonist but whose own limitations become a ceiling to break through
failure_modes: Adults are uniformly villainous â€” protagonist is right about everything, adults about nothing
Adults are uniformly right â€” protagonist's resistance is framed as mere immaturity to be corrected
signals_in_text: Adult characters have legible and sometimes sympathetic reasons for their constraints
Protagonist benefits from adult relationships while also chafing against them
The tension is not 'adults bad' but 'how much of the adult world do I accept as my own'

nodes.label: Identity-Defining Choice Scene
nodes.definition: At least one scene where the protagonist must make a choice that is explicitly about who they are â€” not merely what to do next, but what kind of person they are committed to being. This scene may involve a public declaration, a private decision, or a refusal that costs the protagonist something they valued.
exit_conditions: Protagonist has committed to a version of themselves through an irreversible choice or action
The commitment is legible to the reader even if not articulated by the protagonist
typical_variants: Public act of defiance that declares allegiance to a marginalized identity
Private refusal to compromise values under social pressure
Choosing loyalty to a person or cause over safety, popularity, or comfort
failure_modes: Choice is reversed shortly after with no cost
Character is forced rather than choosing
Scene is narrated around rather than dramatized through action and dialogue
signals_in_text: Scene is given expanded narrative time and interiority
Physical and emotional details accumulate around the moment of choice
Other characters witness or are affected by the choice, making it concrete

nodes.label: First Experience / Crossing Threshold Scene
nodes.definition: At least one scene staging the protagonist's first crossing of a significant experiential threshold â€” first kiss, first act of rebellion, first time they are seen clearly by another person, first confrontation with genuine loss or injustice. The scene must honor the subjective magnitude of the first rather than treating it as ordinary.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is established as inexperienced in this domain
The threshold experience has been anticipated or foreshadowed
exit_conditions: The first experience has occurred and its weight has been registered
The protagonist is demonstrably different on the other side of it
typical_variants: First kiss or moment of acknowledged romantic feeling
First open act of resistance against authority
First encounter with death, loss, or systemic injustice at close range
failure_modes: First experience is underwritten â€” happens quickly and is moved past
Narrator or other characters minimize the experience as it occurs, robbing it of its charge
The 'first' is unclear because prior experiences have been mentioned offhandedly
signals_in_text: Scene slows significantly
Sensory detail is heightened
Interior language shifts â€” syntax or diction becomes more charged than baseline narration
A before and after is visible in how the protagonist carries themselves in subsequent scenes

nodes.label: Confrontation with Authority or System Scene
nodes.definition: At least one scene where the protagonist directly engages with an authority structure â€” not merely chafing privately but in active confrontation: an argument with a parent, a challenge to an institutional rule, a refusal to comply with a systemic demand. The scene must have stakes and consequences.
entry_conditions: Protagonist's need to resist or engage has been built
exit_conditions: Confrontation has occurred with visible consequences
The protagonist's relationship to the authority is permanently altered
typical_variants: Direct argument with a parent where both parties reveal something true about themselves
Institutional challenge â€” filing a complaint, staging a protest, refusing a direct order
Exposure of authority hypocrisy in a public or semi-public setting
failure_modes: Confrontation is deflected before it fully lands
Authority simply capitulates without cost or complication, making the system trivially weak
Protagonist backs down and the story frames this as wisdom rather than defeat
signals_in_text: Power differential is visible in staging and dialogue
Protagonist must pay a real cost for resistance or confrontation
The authority figure responds in a way that reveals their own complexity or limitation

nodes.label: Emotional Intensity and Urgent Sincerity
nodes.definition: YA requires a sustained commitment to the emotional reality of teen experience â€” treating feelings as genuine, urgent, and worthy of full narrative attention. Ironic distance, arch detachment, and retrospective wisdom are permitted only when they emerge from within the protagonist's voice, not imposed from outside. The default register is sincere and present-tense in its emotional commitment.
exit_conditions: Tone sustains emotional availability throughout
Key scenes are not deflected by irony or qualification
typical_variants: Raw and confessional first-person
Close third-person with deep interiority
Heightened and poetic when emotional peaks are reached
failure_modes: Ironic narrator undercuts the protagonist's experience as it unfolds
Tone shifts to adult knowingness at precisely the moments requiring maximum emotional commitment
Emotional intensity is present in some scenes but narratively punished or qualified in others
signals_in_text: Interiority is expansive around emotionally charged events
Prose or dialogue does not pre-ironize the protagonist's feelings
Even painful or embarrassing emotions are treated with dignity within the narrative frame

nodes.label: Condescension Toward Teen Experience
nodes.definition: YA fails when an adult perspective is imposed on teen feelings â€” when the narrative signals (through narrator commentary, adult character pronouncements, or tonal deflation) that the protagonist's experiences are less real, less important, or less worthy of full attention because they belong to an adolescent. This may take the form of ironic undercutting, premature wisdom, or the framing of intense teen emotion as a developmental phase to be outgrown rather than a genuine human experience to be honored.
entry_conditions: Emotional intensity is occurring in the story
exit_conditions: Emotional intensity is sustained without narratorial condescension
typical_variants: Narrator tells us the protagonist will 'look back and laugh'
Adult characters are consistently right and the protagonist's resistance consistently wrong
Teen feelings are described with clinical or ironic distance that drains them of charge
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Narratorial voice shifts to a wiser, older register at emotional peaks
Protagonist's intense feelings are contextualized away rather than honored
The reader is positioned above the protagonist's experience rather than inside it

nodes.label: Static Identity â€” No Formation Occurs
nodes.definition: YA fails when the protagonist arrives fully formed and leaves unchanged â€” when the story is a series of events that happen to a person whose identity is never in genuine flux. The genre requires that the protagonist's sense of self be live material throughout the narrative, shaped by what they experience and choose.
exit_conditions: Protagonist undergoes visible identity transformation through story events
typical_variants: Protagonist defined by a fixed trait (brave, smart, kind) that never wavers or deepens
External circumstances change but protagonist's self-understanding does not
Growth is declared at the end without being dramatized through scenes
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Protagonist's self-description at the end of the story matches their self-description at the beginning
No scenes hinge on who the protagonist is â€” only on what they do
Other characters do not respond to the protagonist differently as the story progresses

edges.label: Promise requires protagonist in formation
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are always partly about who the protagonist is becoming, not just what happens to them
edges.effects_on_character: Character must be genuinely uncertain about their own identity for the story to deliver on its promise
common_alternatives: Some YA stories emphasize the social or romantic dimension more than the explicitly internal one, but identity formation remains the substrate
anti_patterns: Protagonist is fully formed adult-in-miniature â€” genre promise is broken from the first page

edges.label: Promise requires first experiences honored
edges.effects_on_stakes: First experiences must be treated as discoveries rather than repetitions
edges.effects_on_character: Character has not been here before â€” their inexperience is authentic and must be narratively protected
common_alternatives: Some YA protagonists are experienced in one domain but a first-timer in another â€” the asymmetry itself is the story
anti_patterns: Firsts are underwritten because the author has been there before and forgets the reader's proxy hasn't

edges.label: Promise requires visible and tested authority
edges.effects_on_stakes: Authority structures create the external pressure that forces identity choices
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's identity is partly defined by how they position themselves relative to authority
common_alternatives: In some YA the primary authority is peer rather than institutional â€” the popular crowd as the constraining system
anti_patterns: Authority is invisible â€” protagonist faces no external pressure, making their choices purely internal and untested

edges.label: Promise requires choices felt as world-defining
edges.effects_on_stakes: The subjective magnitude of choices must be honored regardless of their objective scale
edges.effects_on_character: Character experiences choices as irreversible and total â€” the narrative must sustain that experience
common_alternatives: Some YA achieves world-defining weight through accumulation of small choices rather than one large one
anti_patterns: Narrator contextualizes choices as 'not that big a deal' â€” deflates genre promise

edges.label: Promise requires emotional intensity and sincerity
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that what happens matters fully and is not to be ironized away
edges.effects_on_character: Character's feelings are the authoritative measure of a scene's significance
common_alternatives: Dark or cynical YA can maintain sincerity within a grim register â€” sincerity does not require optimism
anti_patterns: Detached, ironic narrator treats teen experience as entertainment rather than genuine lived reality

edges.label: Identity formation in the recognizable present
preconditions: Setting is the contemporary world without speculative alteration
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are grounded in social reality â€” what people actually see, judge, and remember
edges.effects_on_character: Identity is formed through real social friction rather than fantastical tests
common_alternatives: YA dystopian
YA romance
YA adventure/fantasy
anti_patterns: Contemporary setting present but social reality has no texture or specificity

edges.label: Authority externalized as systemic oppression
preconditions: Setting operates by speculative or altered rules
Authority is a structuring social system rather than individual figures alone
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes include not just personal identity but the shape of the social world
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's identity formation is inseparable from their relationship to the oppressive system
common_alternatives: Contemporary realism
Adventure/fantasy
anti_patterns: Dystopian system is present but protagonist's identity is not meaningfully shaped or tested by it

edges.label: First romantic experiences as primary formation arena
preconditions: Romantic feeling or relationship is the central emotional engine
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are centered on vulnerability, desire, and the risk of genuine seeing and being seen
edges.effects_on_character: Identity is formed through the experience of loving and being loved for the first time
common_alternatives: Contemporary realism
Adventure/fantasy
anti_patterns: Romance present but does not produce genuine self-knowledge â€” protagonist is the same after as before

edges.label: World-defining choices in literal world-stakes adventure
preconditions: Plot is organized around quest, journey, or fantasy conflict with genuine world stakes
edges.effects_on_stakes: Personal identity choices coincide with choices that affect the entire world, literalizing the YA subjective register
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must choose who they are precisely when the consequences of that choice are maximally public and costly
common_alternatives: Contemporary realism
Dystopian/speculative
anti_patterns: Adventure plot is resolved by external power rather than by who the protagonist commits to being

edges.label: Contemporary realism centers school and social hierarchy
edges.effects_on_stakes: Social standing in a visible hierarchy is at stake in addition to identity
edges.effects_on_character: Character's identity choices have immediate, visible social consequences in their daily environment
common_alternatives: Some contemporary YA is set post-high school or outside school entirely but maintains equivalent social hierarchy
anti_patterns: School setting present but social hierarchy has no effect on protagonist's options or experience

edges.label: Contemporary realism requires richly developed peer world
edges.effects_on_stakes: Peer relationships are the primary social stakes â€” belonging and exclusion are felt daily
edges.effects_on_character: Identity is continuously reflected and contested through peer interaction
common_alternatives: Some YA features a protagonist who is isolated from peers â€” but the isolation itself must be a felt condition, not a narrative convenience
anti_patterns: Protagonist navigates major life events without engaging meaningfully with any peers

edges.label: Dystopian systems function as externalized hierarchy
edges.effects_on_stakes: The system's classifications and rankings are the hierarchy the protagonist must navigate or resist
edges.effects_on_character: Who the system says the protagonist is comes into conflict with who they are becoming
common_alternatives: Hierarchy may be faction-based, ability-based, or caste-based â€” the structural function is the same
anti_patterns: Dystopian hierarchy is defeated externally rather than being meaningfully engaged with as an identity-shaping force

edges.label: Romance is embedded in and shaped by peer social world
edges.effects_on_stakes: Romantic choices have social consequences within the peer world â€” who you love reflects on who you are publicly
edges.effects_on_character: Romance cannot be separated from social belonging â€” the choice of partner is also a social statement
common_alternatives: Some YA romance explicitly breaks from the peer world â€” but the break itself must be a cost that is felt
anti_patterns: Romance occurs in social vacuum with no peer response, consequence, or observation

edges.label: Adventure requires adult world to be left, tested, or transformed
edges.effects_on_stakes: Leaving or challenging the adult world is part of the adventure's meaning
edges.effects_on_character: The protagonist's break from adult structures is the crucible in which their self is formed
common_alternatives: Some YA adventure features a mentor adult who is genuinely helpful â€” but even this relationship must be grown past
anti_patterns: Adventure is completed through adult guidance with no genuine self-determination by the protagonist

edges.label: Visible authority must be ambivalent â€” shelter and cage simultaneously
edges.effects_on_stakes: The ambivalence of authority raises the cost of both compliance and resistance
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist cannot dismiss authority as simply wrong â€” they must navigate its genuine complexity
common_alternatives: Some YA resolves the ambivalence by story's end, but only after genuinely inhabiting it
anti_patterns: Authority is either pure villain or pure helper â€” protagonist's navigation is too easy in either direction

edges.label: Hierarchy pressure forces identity-defining choice
preconditions: Social hierarchy is exerting pressure on the protagonist
edges.effects_on_stakes: Social cost of the identity choice is concrete and immediate
edges.effects_on_character: Character must choose who they are in a context where the social consequences are visible
common_alternatives: Identity choice may be made in private but its social consequences play out publicly
anti_patterns: Hierarchy is present but protagonist faces no choice that tests their relationship to it

edges.label: Peer world is the arena where first experiences occur
edges.effects_on_stakes: First experiences happen in sight of others who will remember and respond
edges.effects_on_character: First experiences in front of peers are simultaneously private (felt internally) and public (socially legible)
common_alternatives: Some first experiences occur in isolation but are brought back into the peer world through disclosure or discovery
anti_patterns: First experiences happen entirely offstage or are reported rather than dramatized

edges.label: Ambivalent adult world must be directly confronted
preconditions: Adult world's constraining dimension has been established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Confrontation puts the relationship with the adult world at genuine risk
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must engage directly with authority rather than only suffering or resenting it privately
common_alternatives: Confrontation may be with a system rather than an individual â€” filing a grievance, organizing a response, exposing a hypocrisy
anti_patterns: Protagonist resists authority only internally â€” no external confrontation ever occurs

edges.label: Identity choice often occurs at or through a threshold crossing
preconditions: Identity-defining choice scene has occurred or is being built toward
edges.effects_on_stakes: The first threshold experience makes the identity choice viscerally real rather than purely conceptual
edges.effects_on_character: Character crosses a line they cannot uncross, making the identity choice irreversible
common_alternatives: In some YA the threshold scene precedes the identity choice and creates the conditions for it
anti_patterns: Identity choice is made but no threshold is crossed â€” everything remains theoretically reversible

edges.label: Threshold crossing provokes or requires confrontation with authority
preconditions: First experience has altered the protagonist in a way visible to or threatening to authority
edges.effects_on_stakes: Authority responds to the protagonist's changed state, making the confrontation necessary
edges.effects_on_character: Having crossed the threshold, the protagonist must now defend or declare the new self to authority
common_alternatives: Confrontation may precede the threshold experience â€” authority's opposition catalyzes the crossing
anti_patterns: Threshold is crossed but authority takes no notice and no confrontation results

edges.label: Confrontation with authority clarifies or demands the final identity choice
edges.effects_on_stakes: Confrontation raises the cost of the identity choice to its maximum
edges.effects_on_character: What authority demands or reveals forces the protagonist to choose who they finally are
common_alternatives: In some YA the confrontation IS the identity choice â€” they are the same scene
anti_patterns: Confrontation resolves without producing any crystallization of the protagonist's identity

edges.label: Emotional intensity betrayed by adult condescension violates genre
preconditions: Emotional intensity is occurring but is undercut by narratorial or adult perspective
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are deflated â€” reader is positioned above rather than with the protagonist
edges.effects_on_character: Character's feelings are implicitly invalidated, making the story a corrective rather than an experience
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid

edges.label: Identity formation required â€” static protagonist violates genre
preconditions: Protagonist faces events but their identity remains unchanged throughout
edges.effects_on_stakes: Without formation, the story is a series of events rather than a transformation
edges.effects_on_character: Character is a plot vehicle rather than a being in the process of becoming
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid
